day1.title: Opening
day1.speaker: Hokila
day1.description: How to make your iPlayground more fun

day1.title: Designing Adaptive UX Through Learning Theory & AI
day1.speaker: Minkyung Kim
day1.description: Every new user is essentially a learner. This session bridges educational science and UX design by applying Cognitive Load Theory, ZPD, and Flow Theory to create adaptive interfaces. It highlights the role of On-Device AI in facilitating these experiences, focusing on its ability to provide immediate feedback, maintain user privacy, and operate without server latency. The presentation demonstrates how these pedagogical principles transform static app interactions into intelligent, personalized learning journeys that evolve alongside the user's proficiency.

day1.title: From Black Box to Glass Box: AI Coding Observability with ccxray
day1.speaker: Justin Lee
day1.description: AI agents are becoming part of developers’ daily work, but what are they doing behind the conversation? Why do they start forgetting context, slow down after a break, or suddenly make one message much more expensive? These issues rarely show up as errors, yet they quietly hurt both developer experience and budget. This talk breaks down the invisible context, cache, and agent behavior behind AI development through real scenarios, then demonstrates how the open-source tool ccxray makes those hidden behaviors observable and offers practical ways to improve them.

day1.title: @ContentBuilder Is All You Need
day1.speaker: UJ Cheng
day1.description: In design systems, we often make components configurable through parameters. As requirements grow more complex, those components accumulate large parameter lists and become hard to maintain. Starting from SwiftUI’s design philosophy, this talk explores how @ContentBuilder can refactor parameter-driven component APIs into content-injection composition, creating components that are more flexible, readable, and extensible while tracing how SwiftUI has continued to strengthen declarative design through WWDC updates.

day1.title: The Bottleneck Was Me: AI-Native Workflows for Large Codebases
day1.speaker: Gyuri Kim
day1.description: AI coding tools excel at small, focused tasks—but struggle when applied to large legacy iOS codebases at scale. The bottleneck is often not the model, but the human translation layer: reading summaries, confirming results, and re-delegating work. Using large-scale codebase analysis for refactoring preparation as a practical example, this session explores AI-native workflow design: machine-friendly structured data for bulk processing, and human-readable reports only for final decisions.

day1.title: Core People: Building More Thoughtful Apps with Apple’s Native Capabilities
day1.speaker: Ethan Huang
day1.description: Apple has Core Data, Core ML, and now Core AI, but the true core of a good app is always people. AI lowers the barrier to building apps, but expectations for high-quality user experiences remain the same. Many features can now be built faster, yet the details grounded in real usage are still what make an app stand out. This talk revisits Apple’s often-overlooked native capabilities, including Accessibility, Localization, Privacy, Notifications, Widgets, and App Intents, and uses practical examples to show how to direct AI toward more thoughtful software.

day1.title: From Swift to Kotlin Multiplatform: Cross-Platform Nitoryu for iOS Developers
day1.speaker: Shengyou Fan
day1.description: Want your iOS app to reach a broader market? Kotlin Multiplatform offers a path to extend iOS development experience and is becoming a cross-platform option worth considering. This session starts with a comparison of Swift and Kotlin syntax, explains the similarities between modern programming languages, and demonstrates how AI-assisted development can improve learning efficiency and reduce migration costs. Using the migration of the iPlayground app as an example, it helps iOS developers understand the advantages of KMP and expand their technical range.

day1.title: Beyond Xcode's Built-in Agents: Discovering iOS Agentic Workflows
day1.speaker: qcl
day1.description: In the AI wave, have you ever wished a coding agent could handle tedious tasks and dramatically improve development efficiency? For iOS engineers, Xcode remains the primary IDE, but its built-in coding agent integration is limited. What can you do when your preferred coding agent cannot be used directly inside Xcode? This session explores several strategies for applying agentic workflows to iOS development: building an intermediary layer so Xcode can communicate indirectly with external agents, or stepping outside the IDE model by using Xcode MCP or even treating Xcode primarily as a compiler while agents drive the development workflow externally. Whether you are an indie developer or part of a large team, you will find paths for AI-assisted iOS development that fit your context.

day1.title: Tweet AI to the Edge
day1.speaker: Bobson
day1.description: “Tweet” AI to the Edge: the next step from localized language models to ambient intelligence. Here, “tweet” is not a social post, but the Chinese idea of pushing, advancing, and deploying. Once open-source communities such as Twinkle AI begin building localized language models, the next question is how to push those models closer to users so they can run on phones, desktops, browsers, and all kinds of edge devices. Little Star began as a mobile LLM tool: from asking whether LLMs could really run on phones, through iOS and Android MVP validation, and toward benchmarking and real-device measurement. This journey showed that edge AI is not only an inference problem, but an end-to-end design challenge across model formats, runtimes, quantization, memory, latency, power consumption, and user experience. Based on Little Star’s exploration, this talk discusses how to bring localized language models to the edge, from model packaging, GGUF, and the llama.cpp ecosystem to on-device benchmarks, context-aware UX, and product imagination around ambient intelligence. It also explores why the value of local models is not just being offline, but being close to users, data, context, and the future AI infrastructure that open-source communities can shape.

day1.title: Why Does Our Swift Code Work? Understanding Swift Through Platforms, Runtime, and the Compiler
day1.speaker: Yuki Aki
day1.description: Swift code looks concise, but every line depends on layers that developers rarely see directly: the platform, the runtime, and the compiler. This talk starts from those hidden structures to explain how Swift operates across different platforms, how the runtime supports and constrains apps, and how the compiler optimizes code. The goal is not to become compiler engineers, but to build a practical mental model that helps us debug, improve build performance, and make better architectural decisions.

day1.title: It's Time! Let's Rewrite All Your RxSwift Code in Swift Concurrency!
day1.speaker: Dex
day1.description: “Your project has used RxSwift for almost ten years. Should you keep using it?” Almost every mature iOS project asks this question. In 2026, the more interesting version is: “Can AI help migrate tens of thousands of lines of Rx code?” This session shares an approach proven feasible with iOS 16+ and Swift 6 from two angles. Technically, it covers which RxSwift patterns now have clean equivalents, which should move to Combine, and which are not worth migrating yet. On the AI collaboration side, it shows how to turn repeated decisions into executable skills and rules for AI agents so mechanical migration work can be delegated. The session ends with a gradual migration workflow validated in practice, plus several real anti-patterns.

day1.title: You don't need a ViewModel
day1.speaker: lovee
day1.description: MVVM is a mature architecture refined over nearly two decades, and it successfully addressed common Fat View Controller problems in the UIKit era. But once SwiftUI redefined UI development around state-driven thinking, do we still need to carry MVVM forward, especially the ViewModel? This talk starts from the design intent of MVVM, examines where MVVM fits or conflicts with SwiftUI, and explains why continuing to introduce ViewModels in SwiftUI often adds unnecessary complexity and can even limit SwiftUI’s strengths. Through practical examples and architectural evolution, we will rethink best practices for the SwiftUI era.

day1.title: Managing AI Like a Junior Engineer: The Discipline Behind Shipping a Solo iOS App with Agents
day1.speaker: Alex Hu
day1.description: AI is writing code faster and faster, so what value is left for engineers? Over the past six months, I used AI agents to build an iOS app that I use every day, with most of the code generated by agents. The app runs and the project did not decay not because of prompt tricks, but because I treated the agent like a junior engineer who needed guidance. Using this real project, I will break down the engineering discipline that keeps agents from drifting or overstepping: ADRs to pin down decisions, contracts to define module boundaries such as using the iPhone as the single data source for Apple Watch, test-first development to protect correctness, and a deferred backlog to control scope. I will also discuss moments where only human judgment could make the call.

day1.title: Navigating the iOS Job Market in 2026: A Senior Tech Recruiter’s Perspective
day1.speaker: Annie Yang
day1.description: Based on the speaker’s seven years of recruiting experience, this session looks back at the ups and downs of Taiwan’s iOS technology market since 2020. From the vantage point of 2026, it analyzes the latest market trends and explores the best ways to prepare for today’s challenges. The speaker looks forward to sharing these insights face to face and helping attendees find steady career momentum amid constant change.

day1.title: Taking Yourself Out of the Loop: Letting Software Upgrade Itself
day1.speaker: tzangms
day1.description: Give an agent three abilities: see the result, inspect itself, and change itself. With those, it can enter a loop: talk to itself, review the result, investigate the problem, make changes, and rerun hundreds of times until it reaches the standard we want. Our job is to define what “good enough” means first.

day2.title: For Agents, By Agents: Building AI Tools That Maintain Themselves
day2.speaker: Rudrank Riyam
day2.description:
Developer tools are no longer built only for humans at a terminal. They are also used, tested, broken, and improved by AI agents.


In this session, I will share the story behind building App Store Connect CLI in public: from an early idea, to multiple job opportunities, acquisition, and keeping it open-source for iOS developers.


I will also show how agent-reported issues, automated reviews, refactors, and release workflows help such tools start to maintain themselves.

day2.title: What Happens After Your App Ships? Automating Marketing, Support, and Product Iteration with AI Agents
day2.speaker: Hana Chang
day2.description: After an app ships, the real problems begin: nobody using it, support emails, social content, user feedback, and product iteration. Using the mojo weight-loss injection tracking app as an example, this talk shares how I built a team of AI agents to handle marketing content, growth experiments, support triage, social listening, user feedback, GitHub issue creation, and product iteration. After launch, AI agents can become teammates that help an iOS app move from “finished” to truly alive.

day2.title: When Touch Fails, Can Your App Still Be Used?
day2.speaker: Vic
day2.description: When users cannot tap the screen with their hands, can your app still be used smoothly? This talk starts with accessibility labels and introduces different aspects of digital accessibility, as well as how assistive technologies actually operate your interface. We will discuss why semantics and control types matter so much for screen readers, and why focus management cannot be ignored in task-heavy interfaces. The talk will also introduce Voice Control and how the system uses on-screen element names to complete actions. Through practical examples, you will learn how to think about and design apps that remain usable in non-touch scenarios and create friendlier experiences for everyone.

day2.title: Lessons from Operating Large-Scale Modular iOS Projects with Tuist
day2.speaker: JungMin Ahn
day2.description:
How to keep a large-scale iOS project maintainable is a challenge many teams face as their codebase continues to grow. Tuist provides a structured way to define project architecture and manage modular iOS projects with clear and repeatable rules.

This talk shares practical lessons from operating a Tuist-based modular iOS project. It discusses how teams can manage project files, demo apps, tests, resources, and dependencies in a scalable way. With the right conventions, Tuist can help turn modularization into a sustainable development workflow.

day2.title: How to Win the Swift Student Challenge
day2.speaker: Thiago Komeno
day2.description:
Winning the Apple Swift Student Challenge is about building something that only you could have built. In this talk, I'll present how a personal story became a winning Swift Playground, and what that process can teach you about code, creativity, and yourself.

This talk will cover three things: how to find an idea only you could build, how to grow your SwiftUI skills under the real constraints of a project, and most importantly, how attention to detail is what separates a good submission from a winning one.

Whether or not you've entered the challenge before, you'll walk away with a framework for turning your own ideas into something you're genuinely proud to submit.

day2.title: Contributing to Apple Container Without Writing Swift: A Cloud Native Engineer's Open Source Journey
day2.speaker: ChengHao Yang
day2.description: Apple open-sourced Apple Container at WWDC 2025 and released version 1.0.0 in June 2026. As an SRE who works daily with Kubernetes and the cloud-native ecosystem, I unexpectedly became one of the contributors to this project. This talk shares, from the perspective of someone who does not write Swift and is not an Apple platform developer, how to get involved in an Apple open-source project: how to choose issues that match your background, bridge language and ecosystem gaps to understand the codebase, and discuss technical solutions with Apple engineers. If you are interested in contributing to Apple open source but do not know where to start, this session shows how to take the first step and what the process is actually like, without waiting until you become the “right person.”

day2.title: How Apple Intelligence Understands Your App
day2.speaker: Joy
day2.description: When a user tells Siri “save this for me” or “add this session to my calendar,” how does Apple Intelligence understand what “this” refers to? App Schema, On-screen Awareness, and Content Transfer from WWDC26 are redefining how apps interact with the system. Future apps will no longer provide only screens and features; they will need to describe their data models, executable capabilities, and content relationships to the system so Apple Intelligence can understand, search, and use their content. If you are following Apple Intelligence, App Intents, or the next generation of Siri, this talk will help you understand the design thinking behind them from a developer’s perspective and the implementation directions you can start adopting today.

day2.title: macOS CLI Magic Commands I Learned from Vibe Coding
day2.speaker: Peter Pan
day2.description: As iOS app developers, we are familiar with Xcode, Swift syntax, and the many frameworks in the iOS SDK, but macOS CLI commands can feel like familiar strangers. While using AI to develop apps, Peter Pan noticed that AI completes tasks quickly through many convenient CLI commands. This session shares the macOS CLI “magic commands” he learned from AI.

day2.title: Age Assurance Frameworks and Digital Identity Wallet
day2.speaker: Denken
day2.description: Starting from Apple’s Declared Age Range API released in 2025, do you know why these APIs are needed? This talk helps you understand the underlying demand for age verification, including international compliance, technical standards, and frontier developments in cryptography.

day2.title: From an Unreadable Japanese Receipt: How a Solo Developer Grew a Side Project into a Product with AI and Feedback Systems
day2.speaker: Ervis Peng
day2.description: On a trip to Japan, I could not understand receipts and wanted to track expenses and settle purchases for others, so I built “TripReceipt,” an iOS app that requires no registration, is free, and has no ads. After I first shared it on Threads, real users started joining, and I began receiving feature requests, suggestions, bug reports, and sponsorship support. This session shares what happens when a side project has real users, and how AI receipt recognition, an in-app feedback chat, push notifications, Telegram integration, and an operations dashboard can form a small product system that continuously hears users, responds quickly, and drives product iteration.

day2.title: Is SDD Just a Slogan, or Can It Actually Work?
day2.speaker: Chien-Long, Kao
day2.description: “Write the specification first, then let AI implement it” sounds ideal, but is SDD, specification-driven development, just a slogan or something that can really work? The common pain of coding with AI is that it has no specification to follow, so it guesses or runs off on its own until we come back to argue about what it got wrong. SDD uses specifications to constrain AI so it does not have to guess and is less likely to drift. In this talk, I will demonstrate an SDD workflow from proposal to implementation to archiving, and show what this approach actually looks like in practice.

workshop_day1.title: In Search of Authenticity in the Age of AI
workshop_day1.speaker: Harry
workshop_day1.description:
In the AI era, more and more code can be delegated to AI. What engineers truly need to think about is no longer just “how to write code,” but “what should humans do in this new era?”

I believe we should spend more time stepping outside the screen, interacting with people, understanding different preferences and perspectives, and engaging with various industries. A deeper understanding of people, industries, and the real world is becoming essential for surviving and thriving in this generation—and it is also much harder for AI to replace.

Through this process, we can develop clearer personal taste and judgment, allowing ourselves to become not merely tool users in the AI era, but creators and decision-makers who differentiate themselves through insight and taste.

workshop_day1.title: Stitch x Antigravity Hands-on Guide: Let AI Turn Visual Design into a Precise iOS App
workshop_day1.speaker: Kevin Chiu
workshop_day1.description:
Workshop introduction
In the traditional app development process, moving from UI design to final iOS code often involves countless rounds of communication, manual layout work, and endless pixel-pushing. This workshop breaks down the wall between design and development and lets you experience a new generation of AI-driven development workflow.

We will combine the powerful AI design engine Stitch with the high-performance development framework Antigravity, and practice turning visual sketches and design intent into production-grade iOS applications. This is not just generating static screens: the design output gains real logic, polished performance, and a workflow that fits developers’ habits. Say goodbye to hand-coding every piece of UI and let AI become your strongest engineering assistant.

workshop_day2.title: I don't know Swift, but I made an AI monster battle game.
workshop_day2.speaker: Hedula
workshop_day2.description: You do not need to be a Swift expert to build an AI-powered iOS game in 40 minutes. This is not magic, but a real example of AI-assisted development. In this session, you will use Xcode and GitHub Copilot to build “Snap Fighter” from scratch: take photos of any objects, let AI generate character names and stats automatically, and battle once you capture two fighters. Along the way, you will see how AI writes code, what to do when it gets things wrong, and which traps it will never proactively warn you about.

workshop_day2.title: Core People: Building More Thoughtful Apps with Apple’s Native Capabilities
workshop_day2.speaker: Ethan Huang
workshop_day2.description:
Apple has Core Data, Core ML, and now Core AI—but the true core of building great apps has always been people.

AI has lowered the barrier to building apps, but users’ expectations for high-quality experiences remain unchanged. Many features can now be completed faster, but what truly makes an app stand out is still the attention to details that reflect real user contexts.

So how do we improve app quality? Apple provides a strong foundation of technical and design resources, from Accessibility, Localization, Privacy, and Notifications to Widgets and App Intents. In this workshop, we’ll build hands-on examples and demonstrate how to guide AI to create more thoughtful software.
